essay:
The cost of intelligence is collapsing, and the industry has not yet reckoned with what that means. This week, MiniMax-M3 eclipsed GPT-5.5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro on key benchmarks while running at five to ten percent of the cost [1]. Pinterest separately revealed it had gutted a frontier model's vision layer and cut its AI spend by ninety percent without meaningful quality loss [2]. NVIDIA released Cosmos 3 as an open omni-model for physical AI reasoning [3]. Three data points, one conclusion: the assumption that state-of-the-art performance requires state-of-the-art spend is breaking down faster than most enterprise AI roadmaps anticipated.

What follows from cheaper models is not simpler decisions but harder ones. If inference costs approach zero, the bottleneck becomes something else — and this week, Anthropic named it directly. Its browser agent was hijacked thirty-one percent of the time before safeguards engaged [4]. A separate analysis found that the real constraint on agentic AI adoption is not model performance but permissions — the organizational and technical gatekeeping that governs what an agent is allowed to touch [5]. Capability is now running ahead of governance, and the gap is where the real risk lives.

Enterprise software has noticed. Salesforce agreed to acquire Contentful this week [6], a move that makes more sense as AI infrastructure than as a CMS play. The logic is straightforward: as AI agents become the primary interface through which users interact with software, the underlying content and data model becomes load-bearing in ways it never was when humans were clicking through UIs. Salesforce is positioning Agentforce not as a feature but as an operating system for enterprise work, and acquisitions like this are the foundation. The company also committed two billion euros to France [7] in a bet that European enterprise AI is a separate market requiring separate investment — a thesis that the EU AI Act is steadily validating.

There is a quieter counternarrative running underneath all of this. DuckDuckGo's no-AI search engine is drawing traffic [8]. The debate over AI psychosis — whether AI systems are producing genuinely disturbing outputs or whether critics are pattern-matching noise into signal — is unresolved and gaining mainstream attention [9]. Meta is reportedly developing an AI pendant [10], another entry in the ambient AI hardware race that consumers have not yet asked for. These are not anti-AI stories so much as they are appetite stories. The market for AI is enormous but uneven, and the companies reading demand signals correctly will look different in three years from those projecting present enthusiasm forward indefinitely.

The most significant event of the week is probably Anthropic filing confidentially to go public [11]. This is not primarily a financing story. It is a legitimacy story. Frontier AI labs have operated with unusual opacity — substantial funding, enormous influence, limited accountability to public markets. An Anthropic IPO changes the information environment around AI safety and capabilities in ways that are hard to predict but likely to matter. The questions that public market analysts ask are different from the questions that private investors ask, and those questions tend to find their way into public discourse. The companies that shaped the current AI moment will now be asked to explain themselves to a broader audience. That is probably good.

essay:
The dominant conversation in enterprise AI this week crystallized around a pointed warning from Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella: companies are "paying for intelligence twice." In remarks that ricocheted across the technology press, Nadella articulated what he called the "Reverse Information Paradox" — the risk that businesses sharing proprietary data with AI model providers are inadvertently training away their competitive advantage. The dynamic inverts the original information paradox, where buyers couldn't evaluate a product without the seller revealing it; now it is the seller — the enterprise — that surrenders its most valuable IP just to access the tool. Nadella suggested firms need protection analogous to patents when contributing proprietary knowledge to AI systems, a framing that should prompt every B2B SaaS vendor and their enterprise customers to rethink data governance before signing another AI platform contract. [1][2][3]

The concern is well-timed. Enterprises are deploying AI agents at a pace that has outstripped their ability to verify what those agents are actually doing. A VentureBeat analysis this week highlighted what researchers are calling an "evaluation gap": agents are acquiring real-world autonomy — managing tasks across email, calendars, and internal tooling — while the governance and oversight layers to audit their decisions lag significantly behind. The numbers are stark: 57% of enterprises have already witnessed AI agents make confident, consequential errors, yet relatively few organizations have an agentic context layer in place to catch those mistakes before they propagate. For B2B SaaS companies building or selling agent-powered products, this gap represents both a liability and a market opportunity: the teams that solve verifiable AI performance first will command a real premium. [4][5]

Into this evaluation vacuum, OpenAI this week launched ChatGPT Work, a cloud-based AI agent pitched at enterprise buyers as an orchestration layer across email, Slack, and calendar systems. The product is a direct response to the "super app" framing that MIT Technology Review flagged — OpenAI making a credible bid to become the operating system of the enterprise knowledge worker, not merely a point solution. The competitive implications for workflow software, CRM, and productivity SaaS are significant. Vendors whose value proposition rests on being the connective tissue of the enterprise stack now face a well-capitalized rival that is explicitly positioning itself in that connective role. The next 12 months will test whether incumbent workflow software can match OpenAI's integration depth or whether buyers begin consolidating around fewer, more capable AI-native platforms. [6][7]

Meanwhile, the infrastructure economics of enterprise AI remain counterintuitive. DeepSeek slashed its API prices by 75% this week, a move that grabbed headlines — but the underlying "100x problem" persists: getting AI models to perform reliably at enterprise scale still requires capabilities orders of magnitude beyond what current systems provide for the most demanding use cases. At the same time, a separate survey found that 86% of enterprises report their GPU infrastructure running at half capacity or less, suggesting that the bottleneck is not compute availability but rather the organizational readiness to deploy workloads effectively. These two data points together describe an industry in a peculiar adolescence: oversupplied on raw capacity, undersupplied on the process maturity and evaluation tooling needed to use that capacity well. [8][9]

Finally, a new class of security threat is emerging from the intersection of AI coding tools and software supply chains. Researchers this week described "slopsquatting" — a variation on typosquatting in which AI coding assistants hallucinate plausible-sounding package names, and malicious actors register those names to serve harmful code to developers who trust the AI's suggestions. For B2B SaaS teams that have adopted AI coding assistants at scale, this is a near-term operational risk, not a theoretical one. The implication is that software procurement and security reviews need to explicitly account for AI-assisted development in their vendor assessments — and that any SaaS vendor whose engineering team uses AI coding tools should be auditing their dependency trees more aggressively than before. [10]
